title: Anger at the Mentor Who Took Credit for Your Work
dek: *The rage that rises when someone in power erases your contribution is not pettiness or ego. It is your body recognizing a violation of reciprocity, a collapse of the mentorship contract, and a theft that goes far beyond intellectual property. This anger has something to teach you about loyalty, about visibility, and about when it is time to leave.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. When a mentor claims your work as theirs, the anger you feel is legitimate boundary intelligence signaling theft and betrayal. Suppressing that response fractures trust in your own perceptions and can lead to chronic resentment or burnout. Naming the anger, documenting what happened, and deciding your next boundary restores agency and prevents the wound from calcifying into bitterness.

She sat in the audience while her mentor presented the framework she had built over eighteen months of field research, her nights, her interviews, her synthesis. He used her exact language, her diagrams, her metaphors. Her name appeared nowhere in the acknowledgments. She felt heat rise in her chest, then a strange flatness, then nothing at all. By the time the applause started, she had already begun the work of talking herself out of what she knew. ## The Particular Betrayal of Mentor Credit Theft Mentor relationships carry a specific gravity. You offer vulnerability, unfinished thinking, the raw material of your emerging competence. The mentor, in theory, offers guidance, access, protection, and amplification. When that person takes your work and presents it as their own, the violation lands in multiple registers at once. First, there is the intellectual theft itself, the erasure of your labor and insight. But beneath that is a deeper wound: the betrayal of trust. You showed them the undefended parts of your thinking. You asked for help. In our experience, the anger that follows this kind of betrayal is compounded by self-blame, by the voice that says you were naive to trust, foolish to share, arrogant to think your contribution mattered. Beverly Engel, who has written extensively on the psychology of betrayal, points out that when someone in a position of authority violates our trust, we often internalize the blame as a way to maintain a sense of control. If it was our fault for trusting, then we can prevent it next time. But this protective mechanism comes at a cost: it teaches us to doubt our own perceptions and to preemptively silence our anger before it can deliver its message. The anger you feel is not a character flaw. It is boundary intelligence. It is your nervous system recognizing that a contract has been broken, that reciprocity has collapsed, that you are in the presence of someone who will take without acknowledgment. That recognition is vital information. ## What Happens When You Swallow the Anger You might tell yourself it doesn't matter, that the work speaks for itself, that making a fuss will damage your career more than staying silent. You might rationalize that mentorship always involves some degree of unacknowledged contribution, that this is just how knowledge transmission works in hierarchical fields. You might decide that your anger is disproportionate, that you are being petty or territorial. All of these are strategies for managing an intolerable feeling. And they work, in the short term. The anger goes underground. You continue the relationship. You keep producing. But something shifts. The work you do becomes tinged with resentment. You start to hold back your best ideas, or you share them in ways that are already armored, pre-defended. You lose the aliveness that comes from thinking out loud with someone you trust. Over the years I've seen people develop a kind of chronic wariness after this experience, a flattening of intellectual generosity. They become transactional in their collaborations, protective of their insights in ways that limit the very exchange that makes creative work generative. The unexpressed anger calcifies into bitterness, and bitterness is one of the most exhausting emotional states to carry. Gabor Maté has written about how suppressed anger, particularly anger we believe we are not allowed to feel, manifests in the body as tension, fatigue, and a pervasive sense of unsafety. When you cannot afford to acknowledge the betrayal, your nervous system remains in a state of low-grade alarm, scanning for the next theft, the next erasure, the next moment when your voice will be overwritten. ## The Body Keeps the Score Sheet Notice where the anger lives when you think about what happened. Does your jaw tighten? Do your shoulders rise toward your ears? Is there heat in your chest, a clenching in your belly, a sensation of wanting to push something away? These are not metaphors. They are physiological responses to a real threat, a violation of the boundaries that keep you safe in relationship. When you replay the moment of discovery, when you remember sitting in that meeting or reading that publication or hearing secondhand that your mentor presented your work without credit, your body responds as if the theft is happening now. Your heart rate may increase. Your breath may shallow. You may feel a wave of heat, or a cold contraction, or a sudden fatigue that makes you want to lie down and stop trying. This is your anger asking to be acknowledged, not acted out, but *felt*. Karla McLaren, whose work on emotional intelligence has shaped how many of us understand anger's purpose, describes anger as the emotion that restores and maintains boundaries. It arises when something or someone has crossed a line, and its function is to help you identify what that line is and what you need to do to reestablish it. If you bypass the anger and move straight to forgiveness, to letting it go, to rising above, you skip the step where you actually learn what boundary was violated and what you need going forward. You deny yourself the information the anger carries. And that information is often quite specific: this person is not safe to share unfinished work with. This relationship requires documentation and witnesses. This dynamic needs to end. ## Naming the Anger, Documenting the Theft One of the most clarifying practices you can do when you discover your work has been taken is to write down, in plain language, what happened. Not the story you will tell later, not the version that makes you sound reasonable or unbothered, but the raw account: what you created, when you shared it, what was taken, how it was presented, what was said or not said. This is not about building a legal case, though in some contexts that may also be necessary. This is about anchoring your perception in specifics so that you cannot later be talked out of what you know. Mentors who take credit are often skilled at reframing, at suggesting you misunderstood, at implying that your contribution was smaller or less original than you believed. The written record is a tether to your own experience. Include dates, emails, drafts, meeting notes. Include who else was present, what feedback you received, what language your mentor used when they first engaged with your ideas. This is not paranoia. This is self-trust. Across the articles we've published on this site, one of the most common regrets people express is not documenting the early warning signs, the small erasures that preceded the larger theft. You might also find it useful to explore [the assessments hub](/assessments) to better understand your own anger response patterns and how you typically navigate power dynamics in professional relationships. Sometimes the anger at a mentor is compounded by older patterns, earlier betrayals, and understanding that layering can help you respond to the current situation with more precision. ## Deciding What Boundary to Set Anger, once acknowledged, asks a question: what are you going to do? Not what you should do, not what is professionally strategic, but what boundary do you need to set so that you can continue to respect yourself and do your work with integrity? For some people, the answer is direct confrontation. A private conversation in which you name what happened, express the impact, and request public correction or acknowledgment. This is a high-risk, high-clarity move. It may restore the relationship, or it may end it, but it will give you information about whether this person is capable of accountability. For others, the boundary is distance. You stop sharing unfinished ideas. You collaborate only in writing, with clear attribution agreements in place. You begin to build relationships with other mentors, other communities, other sources of support and amplification. You do not end the relationship dramatically, but you change its terms in ways that protect your work and your trust. And for some, the only viable boundary is exit. You leave the lab, the program, the organization, the field. This is not weakness. This is recognizing that some environments are structurally hostile to your integrity, and that your anger is telling you to get out before the cost becomes unbearable. The CDC's guidance on stress and the body makes clear that chronic exposure to environments where you cannot safely express or act on your needs leads to measurable harm. If you are experiencing physical symptoms alongside the anger, tight muscles, disrupted sleep, digestive trouble, you might consider [Doctor's Best High Absorption Magnesium Glycinate, 240 Count](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000BD0RT0?tag=spankyspinola-20) to support your nervous system while you navigate this transition. And in cases where the anger feels stuck, where it cycles without resolution, [Plum Flower Bupleurum Dragonbone Oystershell (200 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01LMP3N32?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help move stagnant energy and ease the frustration of unresolved conflict. ## The Anger That Clarifies Your Loyalty One of the gifts of this particular anger, if you can stay with it long enough to let it teach you, is that it clarifies where your loyalty actually lies. Not with the mentor who took your work, not with the institution that enabled or ignored the theft, but with the work itself, with the integrity of the process, with the people who will come after you. When you set a boundary, when you name the theft, when you leave, you are not only protecting yourself. You are refusing to normalize a pattern that harms everyone in the system. You are saying, out loud or through your actions, that this is not acceptable, that mentorship requires reciprocity, that power does not entitle someone to erase your contribution. This is not about revenge. It is about refusing to participate in your own erasure. And that refusal, that willingness to honor your anger and act on the boundary it points toward, is one of the most generative forms of anger there is. It is the anger that builds better structures, that insists on acknowledgment, that makes space for the next person to be seen. You might also find [The Dance of Anger - Harriet Lerner](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0062957031?tag=spankyspinola-20) useful here. Lerner writes about how anger in relationships, particularly in relationships where there is a power differential, can be a catalyst for change if it is expressed clearly and without the demand that the other person change. The boundary is yours to set. The response is theirs to choose. ## A Five-Minute Practice for When the Anger Cycles When you find yourself replaying the theft, when the anger rises and you feel stuck in the loop of what you should have said or what you should do next, try this: Set a timer for five minutes. Sit or stand in a way that feels stable. Place one hand on your chest and one hand on your belly. Close your eyes if that feels safe, or soften your gaze toward the floor. As you breathe, ask the anger: what do you need me to know? Do not try to answer the question with your thinking mind. Just listen. The answer may come as a word, an image, a sensation, a memory. It may be simple: "I need you to stop pretending this is fine." It may be specific: "I need you to send that email." It may be broad: "I need you to leave." Whatever arises, acknowledge it. Say internally or out loud: I hear you. I am listening. You do not have to act on it immediately. You are simply practicing the skill of receiving the anger's message without judgment, without suppression, without the need to fix or resolve it in this moment. When the timer sounds, take three full breaths and open your eyes. Write down, in one sentence, what the anger told you. This is your anchor. This is what you return to when the rationalizations and the fear and the pressure to stay small try to talk you out of what you know. For more practices that work with the full spectrum of anger, visit [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit), where you will find body-based approaches to moving stuck energy and clarifying boundaries. ## What This Anger Teaches About Power Mentor relationships are, by design, asymmetrical. The mentor has access, credibility, networks, institutional power. The mentee has potential, energy, fresh insight, and far less protection. This asymmetry is not inherently harmful. It becomes harmful when the person with more power treats the relationship as extractive rather than reciprocal, when they take without acknowledgment, when they use their position to obscure the contributions of those with less visibility. Your anger at this theft is not just personal. It is structural. It is recognizing a pattern that replicates across fields, across generations, across every context where knowledge and credit are unevenly distributed. Women, people of color, junior scholars, adjuncts, lab techs, research assistants, these are the people whose work most often gets absorbed into someone else's portfolio, whose names disappear from the acknowledgments, whose ideas are repackaged and presented as innovation by those with more access to platforms. When you honor your anger, when you refuse to let the theft be normalized, you are interrupting that pattern. You are saying that your work has value independent of whether the person with power chooses to recognize it. You are modeling for others, especially those watching from positions of even less power, that it is possible to name what happened, to set a boundary, to choose integrity over access. This does not mean the anger will feel good. It does not mean the consequences will be easy. But it does mean that you are no longer complicit in your own erasure, and that matters more than you may yet realize. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### What if confronting my mentor will damage my career? This is a real risk, and only you can assess the cost in your specific context. But consider: what is the cost of not confronting them? How long can you continue in a relationship where your contributions are erased before the resentment damages your capacity to do the work at all? Sometimes the question is not whether there will be consequences, but which set of consequences you can live with. You may also choose a middle path, setting boundaries without public confrontation, building other alliances, or quietly distancing yourself while protecting your work going forward. ### How do I know if I am overreacting? If you are asking this question, you are likely underreacting. People who overreact rarely worry about it. The impulse to minimize your anger, to question whether the theft was real or significant enough to warrant a response, is often a sign that you have internalized the idea that your perceptions are less trustworthy than the mentor's framing. Trust the specifics. Did you create the work? Did they present it as theirs? That is not ambiguous. The anger is proportional. ### Can the relationship be repaired after something like this? Sometimes, if the mentor is capable of genuine accountability. That means not just an apology, but concrete repair: public acknowledgment of your contribution, a change in how they collaborate going forward, a willingness to sit with your anger without defensiveness. If they minimize what happened, if they reframe it as a misunderstanding, if they make you responsible for managing their discomfort, the relationship is not repairable in any form that will serve you. Repair requires two people. You cannot do it alone. ## What Remains After the Anger There will come a time, if you let the anger do its work, when the heat subsides and what remains is clarity. You will know, with a precision that no longer requires justification, what you will and will not accept in your professional relationships. You will recognize the early warning signs, the small erasures, the moments when reciprocity falters. And you will act sooner, with less drama, with more confidence in your own perception. The anger that felt so consuming, so destabilizing, will have done its job. It will have restored the boundary, redefined the relationship, or ended it cleanly. What you are left with is not bitterness but discernment, not resentment but self-respect. You will carry forward the knowledge that your work has value whether or not it is acknowledged by those in power, and that your integrity is not for sale. And perhaps, in time, you will become the kind of mentor who remembers what it felt like to have your work taken, who builds acknowledgment and attribution into every collaboration, who uses their power to strengthen rather than absorb. The anger, honored and acted upon, becomes the foundation for a different kind of relationship, one where visibility is shared, where credit is given freely, where the work of thinking together does not require anyone to disappear. For more on building these kinds of relationships and navigating the emotional labor they require, you might explore the broader reflections at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where we look at power, reciprocity, and the ethics of visibility from multiple angles. You might also consider supporting your system during this time with resources from [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), where you will find formulas traditionally used to ease the physical tension that accompanies prolonged conflict and unresolved anger. The body needs tending when the heart is doing this kind of work. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on anger at the mentor who took credit for your work, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
